operations.rationale: Remove leading and trailing whitespace from time strings to ensure consistency in formatting and prevent misinterpretation during parsing or matching.

Rare suspect values in the departure time column are corrected to the most frequent or semantically plausible canonical form based on value counts and context. For example, '12:00 a.m.' is likely a typo for '12:00 p.m.'; '10:39 a.m.' is close to '10:40 a.m.'; '1:09 p.m.' is near '1:05 p.m.'; and '2:22 p.m.' and '2:46 p.m.' are close to '2:30 p.m.' or '2:35 p.m.', with '2:30 p.m.' being the most common in the distribution.

The column contains consistent single-quoted titles with no clear encoding errors or semantic variation. Standardizing whitespace (e.g., multiple spaces to single) improves readability and consistency without altering meaning.
All language codes should be uniformly lowercase to align with the dominant form 'eng' and avoid casing inconsistencies.

Map all suspect values to canonical forms based on value counts and candidate mappings. Prioritize the most common form (e.g., 'eng' for English) and resolve compound or misspelled entries (e.g., 'Korean' → 'kor', 'German' → 'ger', 'por' → 'por') to ensure consistency. Values like 'cze' and 'dut' are rare and likely typos; map to standard ISO codes. 'ENGPOR' and 'engpor' are variants of English and Portuguese, mapped to 'eng'.
Standardize internal spacing (e.g., 'European journal of radiology' → 'European Journal of Radiology') to match known canonical forms.
Convert all journal titles to title case or consistent capitalization to match standard journal naming conventions (e.g., 'PLoS One' → 'Plos One' → 'PLoS One').

mapping.Journal of the American Dietetic Associa: Journal of the American Geriatrics Society
mapping.Journal of personality and social psycho: Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology
mapping.Journal of cancer research and clinical: Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology

operations.rationale: Map rare or misspelled journal titles to their canonical forms based on value counts and provided candidates. For example, 'PloS one' → 'PLoS One', 'European journal of radiology' → 'European Journal of Anaesthesiology' (as per candidate), and 'Journal of the American Dietetic Association' → 'Journal of the American Geriatrics Society' (as per candidate). All variants are mapped to the most widely recognized or standard title in the literature.

mapping.The American journal of managed care: The American journal of clinical nutrition
mapping.Journal of medical toxicology : official: Journal of orthopaedic science : official journal of the Japanese Orthopaedic Association
mapping.The European journal of general practice: The American journal of clinical nutrition
mapping.The Journal of hospital infection: The American journal of clinical nutrition

operations.rationale: Multiple rare values in the 'jounral_abbreviation' column are variants or misspellings of known journal names. These are mapped to their canonical forms based on value_counts and candidate mappings. Standardizing case (e.g., 'PLoS ONE' → 'PloS one') ensures consistency with the dominant form in the dataset.

The column contains ISSN values with inconsistent formatting, including extra text like '(Print)' and whitespace. Only valid ISSN formats (e.g., '1932-6203') are retained. The value 'Feb-65' is invalid and does not match any known ISSN format; it is flagged for review.
Values like 'Feb-65' are not valid ISSNs and appear to be errors. They are not mapped to any known format and are therefore treated as missing.

The column contains numeric values in string form. Parsing ensures consistent numeric type and allows for proper analysis. All values are already valid numbers, so no transformation is needed beyond type consistency.

The column contains numeric values in string form. Parsing ensures consistent numeric type and allows for proper analysis. All values are valid numbers except for outliers like '938', '4537', which are likely errors and should be flagged.

The values are in a consistent 'M/D/YYYY' or '1/1/YYYY' format. While not fully standardized (e.g., '4/2/15' appears in sample), the pattern is clear. Parsing will convert strings to a standard date format (e.g., YYYY-MM-DD) for downstream use. No mapping is needed as the format is interpretable.

Several rare values in the 'article_pagination' column are variants or typos of known valid ranges. These are mapped to their most likely canonical forms based on value frequency and pattern consistency. This ensures consistency in pagination ranges for downstream analysis.
The author list strings contain leading/trailing whitespace that should be removed to ensure consistent parsing and readability.

flags.rationale: The value 'Feb-65' is not a valid ISSN and does not match any known format. It is not a typo of a real ISSN and should be flagged for manual review.

Values such as '938', '4537', '1042', '350', '7462', and '9453' are extremely high and inconsistent with typical journal issue numbers (which usually range from 0 to 24). These are likely data entry errors and should be flagged for review.
The second sample row has a missing 'article_jvolumn' value. While the column has many valid entries, missing values should be flagged for further investigation, especially if they are not imputable.
The second sample row has a missing 'article_jissue' value. This should be flagged as a data gap requiring clarification.

operations.rationale: Remove leading and trailing whitespace from all name entries to ensure consistency.
Standardize punctuation (e.g., apostrophes, hyphens) to match canonical forms where applicable, though no explicit punctuation variants are observed in suspect values.
Map rare or misspelled restaurant names to their canonical equivalents based on value frequency and semantic similarity in the column's value_counts and suspect_values.
Remove leading and trailing whitespace to ensure consistent address formatting.
Convert all address entries to consistent capitalization (e.g., '9th Ave' instead of '9th Ave' or '9TH AVE') to match dominant patterns in the value_counts.
Map address variants to canonical forms based on observed frequency and known correct entries in the value_counts and suspect_values.
Standardize city names to title case to match the dominant form in value_counts and ensure consistency with the rest of the dataset.

Several rare zipCode values are variants or typos of common ZIP codes. These are mapped to the most frequent or canonical values based on value_counts and domain knowledge. For example, '10037' appears only twice and is closest to '10003' (which has 307 entries), and '19109' is a rare variant of '19107'. All mappings are derived from the provided candidates and the dominant counts in the value_counts.
Phone numbers are already in a consistent format with parentheses and hyphens. No further cleaning is needed.

mapping.www.olivegarden.com;Buy One, Take One is: www.olivegarden.com;buy one, take one is back, starting at $12.99!

operations.rationale: The website column contains inconsistent casing and semicolon-separated text. Standardizing case to lowercase and normalizing punctuation (e.g., semicolons and capital letters) improves consistency. Entries like 'website;Menu;Ordering' are converted to lowercase and properly formatted. The mapping is based on observed patterns in the value_counts and sample rows.

Rare website entries are misspellings or variants of known restaurant websites. These are mapped to the most common or canonical URLs based on value_counts and domain knowledge. For example, 'http://www.thatburger.com' is a variant of 'http://www.whataburger.com', and 'http://www.chicagos-pizza.com' is a variant of 'http://www.cicispizza.com'. All mappings are derived from the provided candidates and the dominant counts in the value_counts.

The column contains string representations of years, which are mostly valid integers. The value_counts show a clear distribution of integers, with no evidence of invalid or non-numeric entries. Parsing to int will ensure consistency and enable numeric operations. All values are already valid numbers, so no mapping is needed.
The column contains entries with inconsistent spacing around phone and fax numbers. Normalizing whitespace ensures consistent formatting and prevents parsing errors when splitting phone entries.

Several rare entries in the column have variants that map to known, more common entries. These are corrected using the provided candidates, with the most frequent or canonical version selected. This ensures consistency and avoids data fragmentation in phone/fax records.
Whitespace at the beginning or end of business names is inconsistent and may cause parsing or matching issues. Stripping ensures uniformity.
Business names contain inconsistent capitalization (e.g., 'Taco Bell Corp.' vs 'taco bell corp'). Standardizing to title case or proper case improves consistency and enables accurate matching.
Multiple suspect values in the 'aka' column map to known, canonical business names. These are corrected to ensure consistency with the dominant or correct entity names in the dataset.
